ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai basil minced beef stir-fry; FOODON_00002473 (beef dish) used as the closest broader class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (beef, minced, cooked) is the closest defensible code; no specific Thai basil stir-fry composite code exists in FoodEx2 hierarchy. Used as closest parent rather than exact match.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of stir-fried minced beef with Thai basil is approximately 400 g, typically served over steamed white rice at a Thai or Thai-influenced hawker stall in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Ground beef, 85% lean / 15% fat, pan-browned, with stir-fry vegetable and sauce composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai basil minced beef stir-fry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central ground beef (85% lean, pan-browned, FDC ID 174032) combined with HPB Singapore sauce and vegetable composite adjustments. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are cross-referenced and retained where consistent with USDA-derived estimates. Energy 162.5 kcal/100g, protein 8.75g, fat 8.75g, carbohydrate 11.25g are plausible for a stir-fry with sauce and basil. Sodium 450mg/100g is consistent with fish sauce and oyster sauce seasoning documented in HPB condiment data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai basil minced beef stir-fry. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed meat-and-sauce stir-fry dishes with moderate carbohydrate from oyster sauce and added sugars. The protein (8.75g) and fat (8.75g) content significantly blunt glycaemic response. Carbohydrate per 100g is 11.25g; GL per 400g serving = (45 × 45.0g carbohydrate) / 100 = 20.3. University of Sydney GI Database consulted for analogous stir-fry and mixed meat dish GI ranges (40–55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 450 mg/100g from fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce; a 400g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78% of the WHO 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce, or substitute low-sodium soy sauce, to bring sodium below 400 mg/100g; increasing vegetable content (green beans, broccoli) also raises potassium to partially offset sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 11.25g/100g is moderate and sugar 2.0g/100g is low; however, the 400g serving delivers 45g total carbohydrate and the accompanying white rice substantially raises the overall glycaemic load of the meal.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should practise portion control on the rice component — substituting brown rice or reducing rice to 100g — and request reduced oyster sauce to lower added sugar; the protein and fat content of the stir-fry itself moderates postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 3.0g/100g and trans fat 0.13g/100g from minced beef and cooking oil; sodium 450mg/100g further elevates cardiovascular risk; a 400g serving provides 12g saturated fat, approaching the 20g daily limit.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150–200g of the stir-fry, request leaner minced beef or substitute minced chicken, and ask for reduced sauce to lower both sodium and saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Minced beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6); chilli is a nightshade ingredient that may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute minced chicken or tofu for minced beef to eliminate arachidonic acid load, request reduced sauces to lower sodium-driven tissue swelling, and monitor symptoms when consuming chilli-containing preparations.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Minced Beef with Thai Basil (Thai: เนื้อสับผัดกะเพรา, romanized: Nuea Sap Phat Kaphrao) is a popular Thai stir-fry dish widely available at Thai restaurants and hawker stalls in Singapore. The dish is prepared by stir-frying minced beef in a hot wok with garlic, fresh chilli, fish sauce, oyster sauce, and light soy sauce, then finishing with generous handfuls of Thai holy basil (kraphao), which imparts a distinctive peppery, clove-like aroma that differentiates it from sweet basil variants. The result is a savoury, slightly spicy, and aromatic dish with a glossy sauce coating the meat. Per 100g, the dish provides approximately 162.5 kcal, 8.75g protein, 8.75g fat (of which 3.0g is saturated), and 11.25g carbohydrate with 2.0g sugar. Sodium is elevated at 450mg/100g due to the multi-sauce seasoning base. A standard 400g plate serving delivers approximately 650 kcal and 1800mg sodium. Clinically, the dish is high concern for hypertension due to sodium load, moderate concern for type 2 diabetes (carbohydrate is moderate but the accompanying rice raises total meal GL significantly), and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat and sodium. It is not suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of red meat. The dish is not vegetarian, not vegan, and not certified halal due to the use of oyster sauce and potential cross-contamination. It is dairy-free and gluten content depends on soy sauce type used.
